incorrects.description: Student selected 5 (items per group) instead of 3 (number of groups)

params.description: Boxes highlight one by one to show the count of groups.

steps.text: You counted what's inside. First count the boxes themselves. How many boxes do you see?

incorrects.description: Student selected 8 (incorrect sum or miscalculation)

params.description: Boxes highlight one by one to emphasize group count.

steps.text: We're counting groups. A group is one box. Count the boxes, not what's inside them.

incorrects.description: Student selected 15 (total items across all groups)

params.description: Boxes highlight one by one to show the group count.

steps.text: You counted all the markers. The question asks how many groups. Count the boxes, not the markers inside.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Boxes highlight one by one as system counts: 1, 2, 3.

steps.text: Let me show you. When we count groups, we count the containers. Watch: one box, two boxes, three boxes. The answer is 3. That's how many groups we have.

incorrects.description: Student selected 3 (number of groups) instead of 5 (items per group)

params.description: One box opens. Items inside highlight one by one as they are counted.

steps.text: You counted the boxes. Now look inside one box. How many markers are in that box?

incorrects.description: Student selected 8 (incorrect calculation)

params.description: One box opens. Items inside highlight one by one.

steps.text: Look inside just one box. Count only the markers you see in that single box.

incorrects.description: Student selected 15 (total items across all groups)

params.description: One box opens. Items inside highlight one by one.

steps.text: You counted all the markers. The question asks how many in each group. Look in one box only.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: One box opens. Items inside highlight one by one as system counts: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

steps.text: Here's how to find it. Look inside one box. Count the markers in that box: one, two, three, four, five. The answer is 5. That's how many are in each group.

incorrects.description: Student set container count to 3, reversed the values (first or second attempt)

steps.text: You put in the 3. But 2 GROUPS means 2 bags. How many bags do you need?

incorrects.description: Student set container count to any value other than 2 or 3 on first attempt

steps.text: Look at the words. How many GROUPS?

incorrects.description: Student set container count to any value other than 2 or 3 on second attempt

params.description: The phrase '2 groups' highlights in the instruction.

steps.text: 2 groups of 3. The first number tells you the groups. That's how many bags you make. Set it to 2.

incorrects.description: Student set container count incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System sets container count to 2. Two bags appear on screen, one at a time.

steps.text: Let me show you. 2 groups of 3. I start with the groups. That's the first number: 2. So I make 2 bags. The number of groups is how many containers you create.

incorrects.description: Student set items per container to 2, reversed the values (first or second attempt)

params.description: 2 items appear in each of the 2 bags.

steps.text: That's how many bags you have. Look inside ONE bag. How many items should be in each?

incorrects.description: Student set items per container to any value other than 3 or 2 on first attempt

steps.text: How many go IN EACH group?

incorrects.description: Student set items per container to any value other than 3 or 2 on second attempt

params.description: The phrase 'of 3' highlights in the instruction.

steps.text: 2 groups OF 3. The second number tells you what goes inside each bag. Put 3 in each one.

incorrects.description: Student set items per container incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System fills each bag with 3 items. Items appear one at a time in each bag until all bags contain 3.

steps.text: Here's how it works. I already have my 2 bags. Now I look at the second number: 3. That tells me how many go inside EACH bag. So I put 3 items in this bag, and 3 items in this bag. The second number is what goes in each group.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 × 4: reversed the groups and items per group

params.description: All 4 circles highlight to show the group count.

steps.text: Count the circles first. That's your groups. How many circles do you see?

incorrects.description: Student selected 4 + 2: confused addition with multiplication

params.description: One circle highlights to show equal groups structure.

steps.text: When you have equal groups, you multiply. The × symbol shows groups of the same size. How many circles? How many dots in each?

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 + 4: confused addition with multiplication and reversed values

params.description: All circles highlight to show the equal groups structure.

steps.text: Equal groups means multiply, not add. Count the circles. That's the first number. Count the dots in one circle. That's the second number.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Circles highlight one by one as they are counted: 1, 2, 3, 4. Then one circle opens to reveal its 2 dots, which highlight: 1, 2.

steps.text: Let me show you. First, count the circles: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four circles. Those are my groups. Now look inside one circle: 1, 2. Two dots in each. So I have 4 groups of 2. That's 4 × 2. The first number is always the groups. The second is how many in each group.
